student_message: Oh cool! The square looks exactly the same at a 1/4 turn because all four sides and corners match perfectly, like a checkerboard tile. What if we stretch it into a long rectangle, does it only match at a 1/2 turn?
final_message:
You are exactly right again, Zara! If we stretch a square into a long rectangle, it only matches at a $$1/2$$ turn because the long and short sides have to line up perfectly. 

Now that you understand this so well, we can start making our very own symmetrical designs! You can deliberately create beautiful patterns by carefully arranging or coloring shapes, like squares and triangles. A design has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after a fractional turn, just like your pinwheel. It has reflection symmetry if a mirror line splits it into two matching halves. You can even arrange the exact same set of shapes in completely different ways to produce designs with different symmetry properties! 🌟

orchestrator_intent.goal: Introduce making symmetrical designs while answering her rectangle question.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The section status is not_started, so we must introduce the new concept. Dialogue is best to answer her question and transition.

current_section_content: You can deliberately create designs that have rotational symmetry, reflection symmetry, or both by carefully arranging or colouring shapes. A design has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after a fractional turn, and it has reflection symmetry if a mirror line splits it into two matching halves. The same set of shapes — such as squares and equilateral triangles with equal side lengths — can be arranged in different ways to produce designs with different symmetry properties. Designs can be sorted into three groups: only rotational symmetry, only reflection symmetry, or both.
session_summary: Zara covered reflectional and rotational symmetry in shapes, letters ('N', 'S'), and digital numbers. She understands these concepts exceptionally well, accurately differentiating between folding and spinning symmetry. Zara exhibited no struggles. Her learning state remains highly engaged and progressive; having mastered basic 2D symmetry, she readily applies concepts to new contexts and proactively asks advanced edge-case questions, such as whether a 6 rotating into a 9 counts as rotational symmetry.
